evidence.title: Management of Acute Pain from Non–Low Back Musculoskeletal Injuries: Guidelines from AAFP and ACP | AAFP
Get NHS help for back or joint problems from community musculoskeletal (MSK) services

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left side lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, night sweats, difficulty breathing, coughing, chest pain, numbness, weakness, tingling, swelling, bruising, or redness over the area. She has no history of kidney problems, urinary symptoms, abdominal pain, recent weight loss, fatigue, or other systemic symptoms. She tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) but it did not provide significant relief.

O: Phone consultation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage area without signs of systemic illness or neurological involvement.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction
3. Intercostal muscle strain
4. Early presentation of herpes zoster (pre-eruptive phase)

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily for 7 days with food
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily for 7 days; Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; advise taking with food to minimize GI irritation; avoid in patients with known NSAID allergy or bleeding disorders
Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, new neurological symptoms develop (numbness, weakness, tingling), fever or systemic symptoms appear, or if no improvement after 2 weeks of treatment
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags or systemic symptoms
